explicit_text: I was born in Coffeyville, Kansas, because it was the closest town with a hospital to our ranch in Oklahoma. Even though I was raised mostly in Oklahoma, Coffeyville was the bigger town where we went to school and church, especially in my early years. My dad grew up near Coffeyville, and I attended grade school there at the Catholic Holy Name school before switching to high school in Oklahoma. Coffeyville was a central part of my childhood experience, bridging my life between two states.
implicit_text: I was born in the closest town with a hospital to our ranch in Oklahoma. My dad grew up near there, and I attended grade school at the Catholic Holy Name school before switching to high school in Oklahoma. That town was a central part of my childhood experience, bridging my life between two states.

explicit_text: Though I was born in Kansas, I was raised my entire childhood in Oklahoma, specifically in Nowata County near South Coffeyville. Our family ranch was in Northeastern Oklahoma, just miles from the Kansas border, and it had been in the family for many years. I went to high school at Linnepah High School in Oklahoma, and much of my life and family history is rooted there. Oklahoma was home to my ranch upbringing and shaped much of my early life.
implicit_text: Though I was born in Kansas, I was raised my entire childhood in the state just south of it, specifically in Nowata County near the border town. Our family ranch was in Northeastern part of that state, just miles from the Kansas border, and it had been in the family for many years. I went to high school at Linnepah High School there, and much of my life and family history is rooted in that state. It was home to my ranch upbringing and shaped much of my early life.

explicit_text: I attended Linnepah High School in Oklahoma after finishing grade school in Coffeyville, Kansas. It was a small school in Nowata County where I played basketball and participated in some activities like 4-H and Future Homemakers of America, although I wasn't very involved in those clubs. Switching to Linnepah was necessary because the Catholic school in Coffeyville did not offer middle and high school education at the time. Linnepah was where I spent my adolescent school years before going to college.

explicit_text: I often think of Reed Drummond when I reflect on my ranch upbringing because her Pioneer Woman cooking show shows her children helping with ranch work just like I did. Growing up, I was outdoors every day helping my dad with ranch chores, riding horses, and doing the hard work that ranch life demands. Reed Drummond's family life on the ranch reminds me a lot of my own childhood experiences in Oklahoma.

Her family life on the ranch reminds me a lot of my own childhood experiences in Oklahoma.

explicit_text: My mother was often called the Martha Stewart of ranching in Northeastern Oklahoma because of her incredible skills as a seamstress and homemaker. She sewed many of our clothes, curtains, and bedspreads, and was responsible for feeding everyone on the ranch, including ranch hands and family. While I didn't inherit her sewing skills, I admired her hard work and dedication to managing the household and ranch life.

explicit_text: My mother's family originally came from Tennessee; her father and uncles moved from there to establish our ranch in Oklahoma. The ranch has been in the family since those early days when four brothers settled the land. So Tennessee is part of my family's roots, even though I was raised in Oklahoma.
implicit_text: My mother's family originally came from the southeastern U.S. state known for its music and mountains; her father and uncles moved from there to establish our ranch in Oklahoma. The ranch has been in the family since those early days when four brothers settled the land. So that state is part of my family's roots, even though I was raised in Oklahoma.

explicit_text: My father, Harry Charles Lynn, was a hard-working man who grew up on a small farm near Liberty, Kansas, not far from Coffeyville. He was a strict disciplinarian who taught us the value of hard work and doing things the right way, especially on the ranch. Many ranch hands who worked for him said it was the hardest but best job they ever had. He had a huge impact on me and many others, and I still remember his specific way of doing things, like opening gates the way he taught.

explicit_text: My dad grew up on a small farm near Liberty, Kansas, which was close to Coffeyville. It was a rural area where he learned farming before moving to ranch life in Oklahoma. Liberty was part of his early life and shaped his work ethic that he later brought to our family ranch.

explicit_text: The Catholic Church was very important in my family life. We attended Mass regularly in Coffeyville, Kansas, and my mother made sure we went to Catholic school even though it meant driving long distances daily. My parents' funeral was held at our Catholic Church in Coffeyville, which was attended by many people, including my father's former ranch hands. Catholicism shaped much of our family values and community connections.

explicit_text: In 2005, my parents were tragically killed in a car accident on a country road while returning from Coffeyville to the ranch. It was a devastating shock for our family, and many people, including former ranch hands, came to their funeral. That year marked a heartbreaking turning point in our lives and the ranch's history.

explicit_text: Oklahoma State University was a college I considered attending because my mother had gone there. My brother also went to Oklahoma State for college before returning to work on the ranch. Although I ultimately chose the University of Tulsa because it was closer to home, OSU was part of our family's educational background.
implicit_text: A major public university in Oklahoma was a college I considered attending because my mother had gone there. My brother also went to that university before returning to work on the ranch. Although I ultimately chose a different university in Tulsa because it was closer to home, that school was part of our family's educational background.

explicit_text: I chose to attend the University of Tulsa because it was the closest college to our ranch and allowed me to come home on weekends. I pursued a degree in education with a minor in journalism there, hoping to become a teacher someday. My family had ties to Tulsa through my grandmother and her siblings, so I was familiar with the city and felt comfortable studying there.

explicit_text: My grandmother, Lila White Lowry, was an important figure in my family. She and her siblings lived in Tulsa, which is why we often visited the city. Her presence helped me feel connected to Tulsa and influenced my decision to attend the University of Tulsa for college.

explicit_text: While I was in high school at Linnepah, 4-H was one of the few extracurricular activities available. Although I was not heavily involved, 4-H was part of the community and school life for students like me growing up on a ranch. It complemented the ranch lifestyle and skills we learned outside of school.

explicit_text: Future Homemakers of America, or FHA, was another club at my high school, but I wasn't very active in it. It was one of the limited options for young women at the time, alongside 4-H and sports like basketball. My interests were more aligned with ranch life and outdoor activities than these school clubs.
